coredata.dc:title: Waste biomass derived nitrogen-rich carbon dots augmented g-C3N4 photocatalyst for valorization of glycerol
coredata.dc:creator: Kumbhar, Shweta
De, Mahuya
coredata.dc:description: The nitrogen-rich carbon dots (CDs) were successfully synthesized by using soyabean de oiled cake as a biomass waste source. This study explored the effects of two green synthesis techniques viz. hydrothermal and microwave. The optimum synthesis temperature was 240 °C, and biomass concentration was 0.05 g/ml. The optimum residence time depended on the preparation method. The higher quantum yield was observed for CDs prepared by microwave method at 78.7% followed by 62.6% for CDs prepared by hydrothermal. CDs synthesized by hydrothermal and microwave method had band gap of 2.48 and 2.23 eV, while lifetimes were 5.97 and 4.62 ns, respectively. The CDs were utilized to modify g-C3N4 to create metal-free heterojunction photocatalysts for photo-oxidation of glycerol. The major product was observed to be glyceraldehyde with a yield of 15.2% and a selectivity of 48%. The catalysts were also effective for oxidation of other alcohols such as ethylene glycol.

UV–vis absorption spectra for CDs synthesized by (a) hydrothermal and (b) microwave methods at different temperatures, (c) XRD patterns of CDs prepared by hydrothermal and microwave methods.

head.ce:title: Waste biomass derived nitrogen-rich carbon dots augmented g-C3N4 photocatalyst for valorization of glycerol

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Guwahati, Guwahati, Assam 781039, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Guwahati, Guwahati, Assam 781039, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The nitrogen-rich carbon dots (CDs) were successfully synthesized by using soyabean de oiled cake as a biomass waste source. This study explored the effects of two green synthesis techniques viz. hydrothermal and microwave. The optimum synthesis temperature was 240 °C, and biomass concentration was 0.05 g/ml. The optimum residence time depended on the preparation method. The higher quantum yield was observed for CDs prepared by microwave method at 78.7% followed by 62.6% for CDs prepared by hydrothermal. CDs synthesized by hydrothermal and microwave method had band gap of 2.48 and 2.23 eV, while lifetimes were 5.97 and 4.62 ns, respectively. The CDs were utilized to modify g-C3N4 to create metal-free heterojunction photocatalysts for photo-oxidation of glycerol. The major product was observed to be glyceraldehyde with a yield of 15.2% and a selectivity of 48%. The catalysts were also effective for oxidation of other alcohols such as ethylene glycol.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Glycerol is the by-product of the biodiesel industry and in recent years focus has been to develop upgradation methods for its conversion to valued added chemicals [1]. Glycerol oxidation has become the most well-known and major approach for converting glycerol to value-added compounds. The main products and their yields vary depending on the rate of the oxidation process, the used catalyst, and the reaction conditions [2]. The photo-oxidation of glycerol is a greener way for selectively producing value-added products. In photo-oxidation process, the use of solar energy and low cost oxidants such as air with zero formation of pollutants render it sustainable and greener [3].

Both UV and visible light active metal (TiO2, Bi2O3, WO3, and Fe containing clay, etc.) based photocatalysts have been reported for glycerol photo-oxidation [3–9]. The described photocatalysts are mostly metal-based with glycerol conversions ranging from 0.7 to 100%. The yields and selectivity of the products also varied. For example, the yield of glyceraldehyde ranged from 2 to 13% while that of dihydroxyacetone was up to 45%. The selectivity of glyceraldehyde on the other hand was reported in the range of 20 to 70% depending on catalysts and process conditions while that for glycolic acid was only 5%. More details are given in . The use of metal however increases cost and toxicity as well as involves stability concerns. The development of metal-free effective catalysts for selective photo-oxidation of glycerol contributes towards making the process greener. The graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) with a band gap of 2.8 eV is a visible-light active non-metal semiconductor with good thermal and chemical stability, making it a potential catalyst for the photo-oxidation reaction. To date, g-C3N4 has been successfully reported for hydrogen generation, dye degradation, water-splitting reactions and sensors [10–14].To the best knowledge of the authors, this is the first time g-C3N4 has been employed for photo-oxidation of glycerol.

The carbon dots (CDs) for their unique physicochemical characteristics, such as resistance to photo-bleaching, excitation-based emission, aqueous stability, flexibility for surface modification, low toxicity, and stable photoluminescence (PL), has the potential of functioning as metal-free greener photocatalyst [15,16]. Usually, organic compounds, such as carbohydrates, carbon rods, L-arginine, and graphene rods are used for the preparation of CDs [17–21]. However, high-purity organic compounds are expensive. Hence, abundantly available and inexpensive waste biomass materials have generated interest as a source for the preparation of CDs. Several studies have documented the synthesis of CDs with high quantum yield and suitable band gap energy utilizing biomass sources. The utilization of abundantly available biomass as a source for fabrication, along with a simple technique, has aided in large-scale synthesis and real-world CD applications. The Cordia myxa L and C. Medica fruits were processed by hydrothermal method to synthesize CDs with quantum yield in the range of 14–16 % with wide band gap energy [22,23]. Similarly, CDs prepared from Tamarindus indica (T. indica) leaves, reported a quantum yield of 46.6 % [24]. The CDs derived from muskmelon peels by the thermal method reported 39.5 % of quantum yield and minimum band gap energy of 3.28 eV [25]. The details on waste biomass-derived CDs are shown in supplementary Table S2. However, attaining higher quantum yield, good optical properties, suitable band gap along with band position, and large charge reservoir are the main challenges for the effective use of CDs as photocatalysts.

The present study investigated the utilization of waste biomass sources for the synthesis of CDs and the effect of process parameters on quantum yield. The use of soyabean de-oiled cake as the biomass source for the fabrication of CDs is reported for the first time. The effect of two facile processes and synthesis parameters on properties of fabricated CDs such as size, band gap, quantum yield, and optical properties were studied and compared. The CDs with the lowest band gap energy and highest quantum yield were selected to form a heterojunction with g-C3N4. The photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4 has been reported to be hampered by the presence of bridging nitrogen atoms in the s-triazine structure that do not participate in charge transfer, and thereby create hindrance [26]. The novelty of this work was the development of metal-free photocatalyst based on novel biomass derived CDs and g-C3N4 (CN) to enhance the photo-oxidation of glycerol to value-added products. The activity of all the photocatalysts was tested under visible light irradiation at room temperature and pressure. The developed photocatalyst gave high selectivity and yield towards glyceraldehyde. The other detected products were glyceric acid and glycolic acid.

Soyabean de-oiled cake (de-oiled cake, Ghodawat Foods International Pvt. Ltd. Kolhapur Maharashtra, India) was the waste biomass carbon source used for the preparation of CDs. Deionized water was obtained from Elix-3M/s Milli-Q, Millipore France (water purification system). The quinine sulfate dihydrate (99% pure) was purchased from Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd, India. Sulphuric acid (98% concentrated), hydrochloric acid (37% concentrated), urea (99% pure), sodium hydroxide (97% pure), potassium hydroxide (85% pure), and glycerol anhydrous (99.5% pure) were purchased from Merck Life Science Pvt. Ltd. All materials were used without any further treatments and purification.

The CDs were fabricated by two different methods. The preparation steps of CDs by both methods and that of CDs modified g-C3N4 photocatalyst are depicted in Fig. 1 . The carbon precursor (2 gm) was added to ultrapure water (40 ml) and stirred well. In hydrothermal synthesis, the solution mixture was transferred to Teflon-lined stainless steel hydrothermal reactor (100 ml) and heated to the desired temperature. The temperature was maintained for a specific residence time. In microwave synthesis, the solution mixture was transferred into a microwave reactor (CEM Discover unit) and exposed to a desired temperature and microwave irradiation for a given time. After completion of the process, a brown-coloured solution was obtained at the end of both methods. This solution was centrifuged at a speed of 10,000 rpm for 30 min for removal of large particles and thereafter passed through a 0.22 μm nylon syringe filter to get pure CDs solution. The CDs prepared by different synthesis methods are denoted in the text as CDH for hydrothermal and CDM microwave synthesis. The effects of process temperature, residence time, and biomass concentration on fabricated CDs were studied. The temperature range of 160–280 °C was used for both synthesis methods. For the hydrothermal process, the residence time was varied from 4 to 16 h, and for the microwave process, it was varied from 5 to 15 min. The biomass concentration was varied from 0.025 to 0.1 g/ml for both methods.

For preparation of photocatalysts, 0.6 ml solution of selected CDs was added to urea solution, which was prepared by dissolving 10 gm of urea in 15 ml of water. The pH was maintained at 4–5 and solution was stirred for 15 min. The prepared mixture solution was dried at 100 °C for 12 h and then thermally polymerized in a muffle furnace at 550 °C for 2 h. The bare CN was prepared by the same procedure without the addition of CDs solution. The prepared catalysts are named as CDHCN and CDMCN, where CDH and CDM represented CDs prepared by hydrothermal and microwave methods, respectively.

The FESEM and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analyses were carried out with Gemini 500 FESEM where the EDX spectrometer was coupled with FESEM. The sample for EDX analyses was prepared by drop-casting pure CDs solution on an aluminum foil-coated small glass slide. The thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA) was used to measure the thermal properties of raw biomass (TG 209 F1 Libra) under a nitrogen atmosphere with a temperature range of 25–700 °C. The CHNS analysis of raw biomass was carried out with a euro vector (EA3000). The morphological studies of synthesized carbon dots were done by field emission transmission electron microscopy (FETEM JEM 2100, JOEL). For TEM analyses the purified CDs solution was dissolved in isopropyl alcohol (1 vol/vol %). The dissolved solution was drop cast over the copper grid and dried for 48 h at 100 °C. The AFM topography images of the CDs were recorded by using the Oxford Cypher instrument and WSxM 4.0 Develop 9.3 image software was used to measure the cross-sectional height profile. Sample preparation for AFM was done by dissolving CDs samples in iso-propanol (1 vol/vol %) and drop-casting the solution on a silicon substrate. Thereafter, drying was done for 48 h at 100 °C. The X-ray diffraction profiles were recorded using Bucker X–ray D8 advance between 2Θ = 10–80° and Cu–Kα radiation was λ = 0.15406 nm. The optical properties (UV–Vis) were measured by UV-2600 Shimadzu spectrophotometer, where absorption spectra were recorded in the range from 250 to 700 nm. The band gap was calculated using the Tauc plot. The value of n was taken as 0.5 for direct gap calculations. The high-resolution XPS spectra was obtained by PHI 5000 versa probe II instruments made by Ulvac-PHI Inc. The photoluminescence (PL) analysis was carried out with a fluorescence spectrometer (HoribaModel: Fluoromax −4C). Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) studies were done with a picosecond time-resolved fluorimeter (Edinburg Instruments, model Lifespec II). For TRPL analysis, 375 nm was set as the excitation wavelength for each sample, with a life span of 50 ns for 1000 counts. The photoelectrochemical (PEC) measurements were carried out using an electrochemical analyzer (CHI1120B). Three electrode system was used, with Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) as the reference electrode, platinum as a counter electrode, and glassy carbon as the working electrode. The 100 µl of CDs solution and 10 mg of photocatalyst were mixed with Nafion binder and water. This solution was sonicated for 30 min and 20 µl of the solution was drop cast over glassy carbon. The electrolyte 0.5 M KOH solution was used for photocurrent and EIS measurement.

Calculation of quantum yield was done by using quinine sulfate as a reference. The quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution has a quantum yield of 54% [27]. This reference was taken based on the emission range of CDs. For this analysis, different concentrations of CD samples and references were prepared. The absorbance of all samples was kept at less than 0.1. The excitation wavelength of 375 nm was used to measure the absorbance and PL emission. The integrated PL emission intensity was plotted against absorbance. The slope of the plot (I/A)S and (I/A)R was used in eq.(1) for the calculation of quantum yield[28].

where ∅ = Quantum yield, I = Integrated PL emission intensity, A = Absorbance at the defined excitation wavelength, n = Refractive Index (1.33 for both sample and reference). The subscript S stands for sample and R for reference.

The photo-oxidation reaction was performed in a 500 ml photo reactor using a 250 W high-pressure mercury vapor visible light lamp. 0.1 M (50 ml) aqueous solution of glycerol was mixed with 0.5 M NaOH (50 ml) solution in equal proportion of volume to prepare 100 ml of feed solution. About 50 mg of catalyst was used for all reactions. Initially, the feed mixture was purged with pure oxygen at a flow rate of 30 ml/min for 20 min and then illuminated by a visible light source for 8 h. After completion of the reaction, the mixture was analyzed using HPLC equipped with a refractive index detector and UV detector at 210 nm. Biorod aminex H+ ion column was used for analysis with 0.1 mmol of H2SO4 as the mobile phase. The column temperature was kept at 60 °C. Based on HPLC analysis the product yield, selectivity, and glycerol conversion were calculated by using the following equations.

The de-oiled cake showed a lignocellulosic fibrous structure. A distinct interconnected structure of fibers was observed (Supplementary Fig.S1). The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) (Supplementary Fig.S1) gave a maximum weight loss of 15% in the temperature range of 150 to 300 °C, which suggested that this temperature was suitable for the disintegration of the biomass. Hence, this temperature range was selected for CD synthesis. The de-oiled cake contained a significant amount of oxygen (34.7 wt%) and nitrogen (10.6 wt%) (Supplementary Table.S3). The primary constituents of waste biomass were determined by CHNS analysis to be carbon (40.9%), nitrogen (8.8%), hydrogen (7.04%), and oxygen (43.3% by difference). The oxygen and nitrogen-rich waste biomass is expected to facilitate the functionalization of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of synthesis parameters on properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 shows the TEM and HRTEM images of CDs, prepared at two different temperatures of 160 and 240 °C, with corresponding size distribution. The residence time was kept constant for hydrothermal synthesis at 4 h and microwave synthesis at 10 min for an initial concentration of biomass of 0.05 g/ml. The synthesized carbon dots were spherical and did not show any agglomeration. The size varied between 3.45 and 5.58 nm for CDs that were prepared by the hydrothermal method, whereas it varied in the range of 3.29 to 3.42 nm for CDs that were prepared by the microwave method. It was observed that irrespective of the preparation methods the size of CDs increased with process temperature. The CDs are formed by the carbonization of organic molecules. The hot spots create the carbon cores by cracking of C–C bond of the organic precursor [29]. The higher temperature resulted in increased carbonization and thereby, enhanced the size of CDs. For the same carbon source at the same process temperature, the size of CDs was smaller when synthesized by microwave method compared to that when prepared by hydrothermal method. The uniform and faster distribution of intense microwave energy throughout the material might have caused faster and uniform carbonization giving a lower dot size as observed. A similar observation was also reported by Anwart et al. [30]. The uniform distribution of energy might also have contributed to the formation of the narrow size distribution for the CDM240 sample prepared using a microwave reactor compared to that for the CDH240 sample prepared in the hydrothermal reactor. AFM analysis also confirmed a lower average height for CDM240 at 3.6 nm compared to that for CDH240 having 5.3 nm. (Supplementary Fig.S2). The height profile of CDs obtained by AFM was identical to the diameter of CDs obtained from TEM images. The HRTEM images (Inset of Fig. 2) suggested crystalline nature for CDs, with lattice spacing in the range of 0.31 to 0.33 nm. These values corresponded to (002) graphitic planes containing sp2 carbons [31]. The XRD patterns of both CDs (Fig. 3 c) displayed the broad peaks at 2θ = 24°. These peaks corresponded to the (002) plane of the graphitic structure of carbon [32]. The corresponding d spacing values were about 0.33 nm for all the samples which was similar to that obtained from HRTEM images. No significant difference was observed in the position of graphitic peaks in the XRD profiles of the CDs obtained by different preparation methods.

The UV–Vis spectra of CDs prepared at different temperatures are shown in Fig. 3. The hydrothermally synthesized CDs showed absorption in the range of 280–600 nm, whereas microwave-synthesized CDs showed absorption in the range of 300–550 nm. The UV spectra for CD samples prepared at different temperatures showed different peaks. For CDH160 peaks were mainly observed at 220 and 278 nm, while for CDH200 a broad peak was observed at 278 nm and a sharper peak at 310 nm. Both CDH240 and CDH280 showed higher intensity peaks at similar positions. The peaks were detected at wavelength of 278, 324, and 360 nm. On the other hand, CDM160 showed only two transitions at 308 and 344 nm. But for remaining three samples synthesized at higher temperatures, three transitions were observed at 285,338 and 378 nm. For both preparation methods, with increase in synthesis temperature the absorption in the visible region increased.

The peaks observed at wavelength below 230 nm corresponds to n → σ* transition involving lone pair electrons of heteroatoms such as oxygen and nitrogen, present in the CDs. These transitions are associated with higher energy [33]. The absorption around 230 to 300 nm may be attributed to π → π* transitions of the core graphitic structure of CDs [34]. The peak exhibited above 300 nm might be associated with n → π* transition corresponding to functional groups such as C = O, having electron lone pair [19]. The high energy n → σ* transition was observed only for CDs prepared by the hydrothermal method at lower temperatures. All the samples showed π → π* and n → π* transitions in the visible region. The higher intensity of peaks above 300 nm suggested higher occurrence of low energy n → π* transitions. The dominance of n → π* transitions for CDM samples suggested that microwave method was more favourable for formation of functional groups containing C = O bonds. This was confirmed by higher oxygen and nitrogen content of CDM samples (microwave prepared) compared to that of CDH samples prepared by hydrothermal methods (Supplementary Table.S3). The negligible n → π* transitions observed for hydrothermal method at lower synthesis temperature also suggested under these conditions formation of functional groups was incomplete. With increase in temperature as concentration of functional groups such as C = O increased; the visible range transitions dominated in samples prepared by hydrothermal as well. Increase in oxygen content with synthesis temperature was confirmed by EDX analysis (Supplementary Table.S3). The XPS study, discussed later, also confirmed presence of higher oxygen containing C = O functional groups in CDM samples prepared by microwave compared to CDH samples prepared by hydrothermal.

The calculated band gap and quantum yield values at all temperatures are tabulated in Table.1 . For the hydrothermal method maximum absorbance was shown at 240 °C with a band gap of 2.43 eV and a quantum yield 27.3%. The microwave process gave a lower band gap of 2.32 eV and a higher quantum yield of 78.7% at similar conditions. Because of the low band gap and high quantum yield obtained at 240 °C, subsequent studies were done at same temperature. The Tauc plot calculation suggested the dependency of band gap energy on the size of the carbogenic core of CDs. As the size of CDs increased with temperature, the band gap energy decreased, with a shift towards the visible range [35]. The dependency of the band gap energy of CDs on their size is explained by the quantum confinement effect (QCE) [36,37]. For other process parameters, the size of the remaining CDs can be predicted based on their band gap value.

Fig. 4 shows the photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of CD samples when they were excited by a wavelength of 375 nm. All the CD samples showed emissions in the range of 400 to 600 nm. The emission wavelength for all the samples showed a red shift compared to the excitation wavelength. This suggested their suitability as wavelength spectra converters that can be utilized to activate photocatalysts. The samples prepared by the microwave method showed a slightly higher emission wavelength than that prepared by the hydrothermal method. Again, the samples, which were prepared at the higher temperature of 240 oC emitted at a slightly higher wavelength. The CDs prepared by the microwave method showed lower emission intensity compared to that shown by CDs prepared by the hydrothermal method, suggesting higher stability of the e- /h+ pair for the former [38]. The analysis thus showed that the stability of e- /h+ pair and emission wavelengths were influenced by the method of preparation of CDs.

The effect of residence time was studied by keeping the process temperature constant at 240 °C and the initial concentration of biomass at 0.05 g/ml. The residence time was varied from 4 to 16 h in the hydrothermal preparation method while in the microwave preparation method, it was varied in the range of 5 to 15 min. The UV–Vis absorbance spectra and corresponding Tauc plot are shown in Supplementary Fig.S4 and Fig.S5, respectively, for CDs prepared using different residence times. For the hydrothermal method, CDs prepared using a residence time of 12 h, gave a minimum band gap value of 2.48 eV and a high quantum yield of 62.6% (Table.1). Similarly, CDs prepared by microwave synthesis showed the minimum band gap and high quantum yield for a residence time of 10 min (Table.1). Hence residence period of 12 h and 10 min. was chosen for hydrothermal and microwave synthesis methods to achieve high quantum yield and low band gap values.

Fig. 5 a and 5b depict the absorption spectra of CDs prepared by varying initial biomass concentrations. The variation in absorption intensity for CDs was noted as biomass concentration varied from 0.025 to 0.1 g/ml. At a given temperature and residence time, biomass concentration at 0.05 g/ml gave maximum absorbance, lower band gap, and higher quantum yield for both preparation methods (Table.1).

The preceding parametric investigation revealed that residence times of 12 h for hydrothermal process and 10 min microwave process resulted lowest band gap and maximum quantum yield by keeping the temperature (240 °C) and biomass concentration (0.05 g/ml) the same for both synthesis methods. The present study gave the highest quantum yield of 62.6% for hydrothermal derived CDs samples CDH and 78.7% for CDs samples prepared by microwave methods CDM, with visible light active band gaps (2.48 eV for CDH and 2.23 eV for CDM) from biomass waste. The current reported range of quantum yield in the literature is 14 to 77.2%, with a band gap of 3.2 to 5.5 eV. (Supplementary Table.S2). The improvement in optical characteristics of present samples might be associated with the presence of nitrogen and oxygen groups in waste biomass sources that facilitated to create defects in the carbon core of CDs [39]. These defects cause the red shifting of emission wavelengths, which reduces bandgap energy and improves quantum yield [40].

The elemental analysis reveals that (Supplementary Table.S3) carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen were the main elements of the CDs fabricated by both synthesis methods. The oxygen content varied in the range of 41.9–43.5 wt%. The higher oxygen content of CDM suggested better retainment of oxygen-containing functional groups. The higher nitrogen content of 10.4–12.2 wt% might have resulted from the presence of nitrogen in the source material.

Fig. 6 compares the XPS spectra of CDs prepared by hydrothermal and microwave methods. The survey spectra of CDs samples, as shown in Fig. 6a, confirmed the presence of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen in both the samples. The C1s spectra (Fig. 6 b and c) showed three distinct peaks at 284.4, 285.1, and 287.9 eV for CDH samples, whereas peaks at 284.6, 285.8, and 287.9 eV were observed for CDM samples. These peaks might be attributed to C–C, C-OH/C-O-C, and C = O [41]. The three peaks of N1s spectra (Fig. 6 d and e) were located at 399.1,399.7 and 400.2 eV for CDH and at 398.2, 400, and 400.2 eV for CDM samples corresponding to amine/amide group (C = N), pyrrolic (C-NH-/ O = C-NH-) and graphitic(C-N-C) nitrogen groups, respectively [42,43], confirming the presence of different nitrogen-containing functional groups. Similarly, the O1s spectra (Fig. 6 f and g) corresponding to the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups showed two peaks at 531.2 and 532.2 eV for CDH sample and at 532.2 and 533.2 eV for CDM samples. These peaks corresponded to C = O and C-OH/C-O-C, respectively [44].

The higher intensity of N1s and O1s spectra was observed for CDs prepared by microwave method compared to that of prepared by hydrothermal method. Relative content of different oxygen and nitrogen containing functional groups are tabulated in Table 2 . It can be observed that concentration of C = O and C = N groups were higher in samples prepared by microwave method which might have caused dominance of n → π* transitions for these samples as discussed earlier for UV analysis (Fig. 3). This observation is also in good agreement with EDX results which showed higher oxygen content for CDs prepared by microwave method as discussed in earlier section. The variation in functional group content suggested development of distinct structures for CDs when prepared by different methods, in spite of using same carbon precursor.

Fig. 7 a compares the TRPL plot and the calculated average time decay values are listed in Table 3 . The CDs, prepared by the hydrothermal method gave a longer lifetime of 5.97 ns than that prepared by the microwave process (4.62 ns). The longer lifetime is associated with the trap state emission of an electron [45]. Hence, for the CDs prepared by the hydrothermal method, the longer trapping of electrons facilitated the prevention of the recombination of electron and hole pairs. The Mott-Schottky analysis (Fig. 7b) was used to predict the respective band alignment of CD samples. The positive slope of all CD samples suggested the n-type behavior of semiconductors [46]. It was observed that the valance band potential (VFB) of CDs depended on the preparation method and the values were −0.31 and −0.40 V Vs Ag/AgCl, for CDH and CDM samples, respectively. These corresponded to −0.11, and −0.20 V w.r.t to NHE (ENHE = EAg/AgCl + 0.197 V)[47].The conduction band (ECB) is predicted at 0.1 V more negative than VFB [48]. According to this relation, ECB can be predicted as −0.21, and −0.30 V (Table 3) for CDH and CDM respectively. The lower slope of the MS plot for CDH suggested a higher charge density [49].

The CDs sample with low band gap value and high quantum yield was selected to prepare a heterojunction photocatalyst with g-C3N4. The CDs that were synthesized at a temperature of 240 °C with initial biomass concentration of 0.05 g/ml and a residence time of 12 h for the hydrothermal preparation method and 10 min for microwave method fulfilled the above conditions.The CDs synthesized in these conditions were used to prepare hetero-junction photocatalysts with graphitic carbon nitride (CDCN) as shown in Fig. 1.

Fig. 8 depicts the structural and optical properties of CDCN photocatalysts. The FETEM images of prepared photocatalysts (Fig. 8a and 8b) showed the evenly spaced CDs on the sheets of CN. No agglomeration of CDs was observed in any samples. Fig. 8c demonstrates the XRD patterns of bare CN and CDCN photocatalysts. The XRD graph of CN consisted of two signature peaks at 13.5° and 27.3°, assigned to repetitive tri-s-triazine units and stacked aromatic structures, respectively [26]. In CDCN photocatalysts, these two-diffraction peaks were retained with an additional small broad peak at 21.2°. Since this peak was not observed in the bare CN sample, it can be assumed that the peaks appeared due to the presence of CDs in the photo-catalysts and may be assigned to the graphitic peak of CDs. The XRD pattern of pure CDs also confirmed the presence of graphite peaks in the same range (Fig. 3c).

Fig. 8d gives the UV–Vis absorption profiles for the prepared CDCN photocatalysts. The presence of CDs caused enhancement in the absorption capacity of bare CN, both in UV and visible regions. The bare CN and CDMCN, both had absorption edges at 450 nm. For the CDHCN sample, the absorbance edge experienced a redshift to 470 nm. The Tauc plot (Fig. 8e) showed a decrease in band gap energy from 2.78 eV for bare CN to 2.48 and 2.62 eV for CDHCN and CDMCN, respectively. The PL intensity of CDCN photocatalysts was much lower than bare CN which indicated the slower electron-hole recombination rate for the former [50,51]. The minor shift of emission wavelengths confirmed strong interaction and heterojunction formation between CD and CN in the composite (Fig. 8f).

Fig. 8g shows the chronoamperometry studies for samples carried out under visible light irradiation. The CDM showed a higher photocurrent density than CDH which might be attributed to a higher quantum yield for the former. The photocurrent density was higher for photocatalysts compared to that of pure CDs or bare CN. The highest photocurrent density was obtained for CDHCN followed by that of CDMCN. The increase in photocurrent density in composite photocatalysts might be attributed to the formation of a heterojunction that promoted charge carrier movement [52]. Higher photocurrent density can be correlated with higher availability of electrons for photoreaction, thereby giving better photocatalytic performance [26,53]. Fig. 8h displays the EIS Nyquist plot for the photocatalysts. The reduced arc represented low resistance to charge transfer between CN and CDs [54]. The lower resistance of CDHCN may promote charge transfer efficiency and conductivity [38].

Fig. 9 a and 9b show the yield and selectivity, respectively, of the products for photo-oxidation of glycerol over CDCN photocatalysts. The glyceric acid, glycolic acid, and glyceraldehyde were detected in considerable amounts for both catalysts. For both the CDCN catalysts, the glyceraldehyde yield was highest followed by that of glyceric acid and glycolic acid. The yield of oxalic was the lowest for all the catalysts.

For CDHCN, the yield was higher for all the products (glyceraldehyde 15.2 %, glyceric acid 8.9 %, and glycolic acid 7.9 %) compared to that obtained for CDMCN (glyceraldehyde 10 %, glyceric acid 8.2 %, and glycolic acid 5.8%). Both the composite catalysts showed higher selectivity for glyceraldehyde and glycolic acid (Fig. 9b). The highest selectivity for glyceraldehyde was obtained for CDHCN at 48% and it was 45% for CDMCN. The CDMCN gave slightly higher selectivity for glyceric acid at 34% compared to that of CDHCN catalyst (30%). The selectivity of glycolic acid for CDMCN and CDHCN catalysts was 20 and 23%, respectively. The selectivity of glyceraldehyde, glyceric acid, and glycolic acid were 30, 30, and 18% for bare CN, lower in comparison to the modified photocatalysts. The overall conversion of glycerol calculated by eq. (2), was 17.3% for bare CN, which increased to 33.2% for CDHCN and 23.6% for CDMCN photocatalysts. The yield for all products was higher for composite catalysts containing hydrothermally generated CDs. For both catalysts, yield, and selectivity were highest for glyceraldehyde. The selectivity for oxalic acid was lower for composite catalysts than that for bare CN. This suggested that the presence of CDs in catalysts suppressed oxidation and increased the yield of intermediate value-added products.

Fig. 9c shows the possible reaction pathway for the catalysts. The primary alcoholic group might have initiated the photo-oxidation process to give glyceraldehyde which on further photo-oxidation yielded glyceric acid. Through C–C bond cleavage, glyceraldehyde and glyceric acid produced the glycolic acid with the loss of carbon. The glycolic acid further can be oxidized to oxalic acid which was also detected in the final reaction mixture. For metal-based photocatalysts, yield and selectivity of glyceraldehyde were recorded in the ranges of 2–13% and 20–70%, respectively. However, results for the other two products are less reported. The performance of prepared non-metal based catalysts was better or at par compared to that reported for metal-based catalysts. The comparison with a few reported studies has been shown in Supplementary Table.S1.

The flat band potential for CN was obtained at −0.87 V (Vs. Ag/AgCl). The Mott-Schottky analysis of CN is shown in supplementary Fig. S6b. According to the Mott-Schottky predictions, the conduction band position for CN would be at −0.77 V (Vs. NHE). Fig. 9d shows the relative positions of bands of CD and CN for CDHCN. In the presence of visible light, the CDs present in CDCN photocatalyst are expected to be activated preferentially due to their lower band gap. The electron-hole pairs (e - / h +) are generated as per eq.5.

The holes (h +) generated at VB of CDs and CN may react with hydroxyl ions, which are available abundantly due to the presence of NaOH in the reaction mixture. The OH– ions reacts with holes generated from the photocatalyst at 2.00 eV to form hydroxyl radicals (eq.6). The VB of CDH is more positive than CN. The holes of CDH get transferred to VB of CN due to the favourable potential difference between VB of CN and CDH. This transition stabilizes the e-/h+ pairs to suppress the recombination of pairs (e - and h +). The electrons (e -) from CB of CN and purged oxygen gas generate superoxide radicals at −0.33 eV (eq.7). The oxidation of water at 0.82 eV generates protons (eq.8), which are combined with superoxide radicals to form hydrogen peroxide (eq.9). The process of formation of H2O2 was formed at 1.81 eV on further dissociation of hydrogen peroxide in presence of light form hydroxyl radicals and ion (eq.10). The superoxide radicals and hydroxyl radicals are the actual reactive oxygen species which are responsible to initiate photo-oxidation of glycerol [6]. The excess supply of hydroxyl radicals, facilitated by the presence of NaOH, helped to boost the photooxidation reaction. The role of NaOH and purged oxygen was confirmed by the fact that when the reaction was carried out either in the absence of NaOH or oxygen, the conversion was negligible. The relative band positions of CN and CDH were suitable and facilitated the occurrence of all the above reactions. This formation of heterojunction between CDH and CN facilitated the mobility of charge and thereby reduced recombination of charges. However, for CN and CDM samples, the VB potential of CDM (1.93 V) was not suitable for the generation of hydroxyl radicals (supplementary Fig. S6c). This limited generation of reactive oxygen species might have contributed to the lower performance of CDMCN photocatalyst.

The photochemical reactions depends the concentration of the reactant as well as on intensity of the light [55]. The intensity of irradiated light remained constant and in abundance during reaction time. Hence the present photocatalytic reaction can be represented by a pseudo-first-order rate expression as shown in equation (11) [56].
(11)
ln
C 0
C t
= k t

The k is the apparent rate constant while t is the irradiation time. The C0 is concentration of glycerol at time t = 0 and Ct is the concentration of glycerol at the irradiation time t. Fig. 10 shows the ln (Co/Ct) vs time plot. A good liner fitting was observed with a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.995. The reaction rate constant was determined from the slope of plot as 0.068 h−1.

The photo-oxidation of ethylene glycol was carried out at similar reaction conditions to validate the activity of photocatalysts for other alcohols. The corresponding yield and selectivity of products are shown in Fig. 11 . The glyoxylic acid was the main product observed for all photocatalysts (Fig. 11a). The yield of glyoxylic acid was highest for CDHCN (29 %) followed by CDMCN (16.3 %) and CN (13%). The formation of other products such as oxalic acid and glycolic acid, was much lower compared to that of glyoxylic acid. The CDHCN photocatalyst accordingly demonstrated the highest selectivity for glyoxylic acid at 92.8 %; whereas CDMCN showed selectivity of 82.4 % (Fig. 11b). The CN showed the least selectivity of 80%. All the photocatalysts illustrated the lower selectivity for glycolic acid and oxalic acid. The total conversion of ethylene glycol was 16.3 % for bare CN, which increased to 31.2 % for CDHCN and 19.8 % for CDMCN photocatalysts. Overall, it was observed that the CDHCN had higher photocatalytic activity for photo-oxidation of ethylene glycol.

The comparison of photo-oxidation results of glycerol and ethylene glycol showed that though the product distribution was different in two cases, but in both processes photocatalytic activity was highest for composite photocatalyst CDHCN, prepared by hydrothermally method. It can be also noted that the conversions for composite catalysts were similar for photo-oxidation of both glycerol and ethylene glycol (in range of 20–30%). However, selectivity of the main product (glyoxylic acid) was much higher for photo-oxidation of ethylene glycol. Consequently quite high yield was obtained for the same.

In this study, simple and cost-effective hydrothermal and microwave methods were used to synthesize carbon dots (CDs) from biomass waste such as soyabean de-oiled cake. The influence of process parameters including temperature, residence time, and initial biomass concentration on the optical, structural, and morphological properties of CDs was investigated. The size of CDs varied between 3.2 and 5.5 nm with the band gap energy in the range of 2.23 to 2.71 eV. Maximum quantum yield, lowest band gap, and maximum stabilization of e-/h+ pair were obtained at a temperature of 240 °C, initial biomass concentration of 0.05 g/ml, and residence time of 12 h for hydrothermal method and 10 min for microwave method. The maximum quantum yield was observed at 78.7% for microwave synthesized samples, whereas 62.6% for the CDs produced by the hydrothermal method. The visible light active band gap was found to be 2.23 eV for microwave manufactured samples and 2.48 eV for hydrothermal CDs. The CDs prepared by the hydrothermal approach showed a greater lifetime of 5.97 ns, compared to that observed for CDs prepared by microwave method at 4.92 ns. These results suggested that the CDs fabricated by different synthesis methods develop distinct band structures with different trapping states. When these CDs were used to form a heterojunction with g-C3N4 and resultant non-metals based photocatalysts showed higher activity for photo-oxidation of glycerol under visible light irradiation. The glyceraldehyde was the main product with a yield of 15.2% and selectivity of 48%. Simultaneously yield of glycolic and glyceric acid was noted. The high absorbance in the visible light range, narrow band gap energy, low PL intensity, high photocurrent density, minimal resistance to charge transfer, and appropriate band positions make CDHCN a suitable catalyst to perform well in photo-oxidation reactions. The presence of CDs in catalysts was observed to suppress complete oxidation and increased the yield of intermediate value-added products. However, the prepared photocatalyst gave limited conversion of glycerol which might be attributed to limited charge availability on CDs. Further functionalization of CDs may results in improvement in their charge availability and subsequent catalytic performance. The CDHCN and CDMCN photocatalysts were also observed to be active for photo-oxidation of ethylene glycol, but gave different product distribution.
